flashcards.back: One-way communication where devices can only send or receive (e.g., keyboard to computer).

flashcards.front: Which device operates at the physical layer?
flashcards.back: Hub - regenerates and broadcasts signals to all connected devices.
Full-duplex allows simultaneous two-way communication (e.g., telephone), while half-duplex requires alternation (e.g., walkie-talkie).

flashcards.front: What is the primary function of a router?
flashcards.back: Routes data between different networks using IP addresses at the network layer (Layer 3).

flashcards.front: Which transmission mode is used in Ethernet networks?
flashcards.back: Full-duplex in modern switched networks, half-duplex in legacy hub-based networks.

flashcards.front: What layer do switches operate at?
flashcards.back: Data Link layer (Layer 2) using MAC addresses for forwarding decisions.
Collision Detection protocol used in half-duplex Ethernet networks to manage data collisions.

flashcards.front: What's the key difference between bridge and switch?
flashcards.back: Bridges have fewer ports and software-based forwarding, switches have more ports and hardware-based ASIC forwarding.
Repeater - amplifies signals to overcome distance limitations in physical media.
Network segment where data packets can collide with each other (reduced by switches).
Network Interface Card - hardware connecting devices to a network.

flashcards.front: What device converts digital to analog signals?
